var_functions.query_db:2.description: ["About the Author", "SANDRA WILDE, Ph.D., is widely recognized for her expertise in developmental spelling and her advocacy of holistic approaches to spelling and phonics. She is Professor of Curriculum and Instruction at Portland State University in Oregon. She is best known for her work in invented spelling, phonics and miscue analysis. She specializes in showing teachers how kids' invented spellings and miscues can help us work with them in more sophisticated and learner-centered ways. Looking at what kids do as they read and write is at the heart of Sandra's presentations and workshops. She can do lively keynote presentations that highlight the interesting things that we can learn by paying close attention to students' invented spellings and miscues, as well as workshops of varying lengths that focus on student-centered teaching of spelling and phonics. She has recently begun offering workshops that focus on understanding students' miscues as a guide to appropriate instruction, p"]

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:6.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:2.description: ["About the Author", "SANDRA WILDE, Ph.D., is widely recognized for her expertise in developmental spelling and her advocacy of holistic approaches to spelling and phonics. She is Professor of Curriculum and Instruction at Portland State University in Oregon. She is best known for her work in invented spelling, phonics and miscue analysis. She specializes in showing teachers how kids' invented spellings and miscues can help us work with them in more sophisticated and learner-centered ways. Looking at what kids do as they read and write is at the heart of Sandra's presentations and workshops. She can do lively keynote presentations that highlight the interesting things that we can learn by paying close attention to students' invented spellings and miscues, as well as workshops of varying lengths that focus on student-centered teaching of spelling and phonics. She has recently begun offering workshops that focus on understanding students' miscues as a guide to appropriate instruction, p"]

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:6.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

args.code:
import json
import re

# Get the file path from the storage variable
file_path = var_functions.query_db_14

# Open and read the JSON file
with open(file_path, 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

def extract_year_from_details(details_text):
    """Extract publication year from details text"""
    if not details_text:
        return None
        
    patterns = [
        r'released on [A-Za-z]+ \d+, (\d{4})',
        r'first edition on [A-Za-z]+ \d+, (\d{4})',
        r'published on [A-Za-z]+ \d+, (\d{4})',
        r'published by [A-Za-z0-9 &,]+ on ([A-Za-z]+ \d+, )?(\d{4})',
        r'published independently on [A-Za-z]+ \d+, (\d{4})',
        r'in its (first|second|third|2013th|revised|supplement) edition on ([A-Za-z]+ \d+, )?(\d{4})',
        r'in its (first|second|third|2013th|revised|supplement|reprint) edition (in|on) ([A-Za-z]+ )?(\d{4})',
        r'(January|February|March|April|May|June|July|August|September|October|November|December) (\d{1,2}), (\d{4})',
        r'\b(19\d{2}|20\d{2})\b'  # Fallback: any 4-digit year starting with 19 or 20
    ]
    
    # Try each pattern
    for pattern in patterns:
        match = re.search(pattern, details_text)
        if match:
            try:
                # Find the last group that looks like a 4-digit year
                groups = match.groups()
                for group in reversed(groups):
                    if group and len(group) == 4 and group.isdigit():
                        year = int(group)
                        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
                            return year
            except:
                continue
    
    return None

# Extract publication years
books_with_years = []
for book in books_data:
    year = extract_year_from_details(book.get('details', ''))
    if year:
        books_with_years.append({
            'book_id': book['book_id'],
            'publication_year': year
        })

# Show some examples
result = {
    'total_books': len(books_data),
    'books_with_years': len(books_with_years),
    'sample_books': books_with_years[:10]
}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.", "review_time": "2012-11-24 18:52:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_186"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "Four Stars", "text": "Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.", "review_time": "2015-12-31 13:35:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "1", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_191"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "A wonderful adventure in France", "text": "I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. result.preview:
[{"rating": "4", "title": "Ha! On me! I thought this was a cookbook!", "text": "Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are \"Tasting Notes\" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.", "review_time": "2012-11-24 18:52:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_186"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "Four Stars", "text": "Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.", "review_time": "2015-12-31 13:35:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "1", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_191"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "A wonderful adventure in France", "text": "I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!", "review_time": "2013-05-05 10:47:00", "helpful_vote": "1", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_190"}, {"rating": "5", "title": "Best beginner book. Been looking for something like this for a long time.", "text": "Looked online for years for something like this. It's the best I've seen.", "review_time": "2020-08-12 11:06:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "1", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_8"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "Referance Guide", "text": "Good reference guide for the basics", "review_time": "2014-11-13 18:55:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "1", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_178"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "A Good read for Meat Eaters, and Veggie Heads as well", "text": "We live in an area where most of our neighbors hunt. Although we've been here for 20 years, I would say that it's only been within the past five years that this former city girl has embraced the country lifestyle. That lifestyle includes hunting, something that I've almost lost some city friends over.

My feelings about hunting are as follows...although I myself eat very little meat, my dogs are my family, and what can I say, it's a family of carnivores! In addition, when you are aware of the dark side of those pretty plastic wrapped packages of meat in the supermarket, hunting seems pretty darn humane, and a reasonable way to procure meat for your family. I think of what the cows and chickens made into dog kibble went through, and what diseases they might have had before being in a can of cat food.

That being said, I'm always interested in different perspectives about hunting, lifestyles, and philosophies.

I enjoyed this book quite a bit because it contains all of the above. There seem to be so many books about hunting, magazines about hunting. Books on bow hunting, and dialogue about hunting a mule deer as opposed to a white tailed deer. I felt like this book fills a gap that had not been covered in any of the articles or books I had previously read.

Because I am passionate about knowing where my food comes from, and doing even a small part to make choices that are responsible, it was refreshing and affirming to read the author's perspective. When we grab a fast food hamburger, we don't think about the suffering animal that probably led an unnatural life, and we certainly don't think of the workers in the processing plants that do a job so brutal and often dangerous. This book reminds us to respect the food we eat, something that comes more naturally when you are closer to your food, closer to the animal that gives its life.", "review_time": "2013-02-20 16:09:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_186"}, {"rating": "5", "title": "Greet book", "text": "Lots of great information. Many projects to make. Easy instructions. Love the patterns. Plan on making most of the projects.", "review_time": "2020-02-27 05:11:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_76"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "For anyone except avid non-hunters.", "text": "This is a strange book. It's basically a series of hunting short stories that also seek to explain why this guy likes hunting and why some people are so squeamish about it these days. It's strange because - having grown up in rural America - I forget that some people (including men) don't like hunting, and I remember thinking to myself often throughout the book that most of the stories didn't seem all that different from what I could hear about from anyone I grew up with. But now this guy is living in NYC, so I can imagine how he's now surrounded by people so far removed from his reality that they think he's a barbarian (similar to my Jersey-raised brother-in-law who once said to me in surprise, \"they let you have guns!?!?!\", as if he grew up under some communist regime rather than in the Land of the Free).

Despite all this, it seems as if he is writing mostly for other hunters. And I want to be clear that he is a very talented writer and it was very easy to enjoy this book, even not being an active hunter myself. He reminds me a little bit of myself in that he can write and explain his feelings eloquently, and yet if you don't like him without justification, he'll become self righteous quickly and give you more reasons to dislike him. You'll see his writing have spots of crudeness at times, which allows you to see where he grew up.

None of these are meant to be negative. I'm just trying - perhaps imperfectly - to point out why this is a strange book. All the more reason to read it.", "review_time": "2013-01-06 07:52:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_186"}, {"rating": "5", "title": "Highly recommend this book if you love history of Mid Atlantic wrestling...", "text": "This was an Awesome look at History of Crockett promotions title belts...Pic were very coo

args.code:
import json
import re

# We need to access the file path. Based on the pattern, it should be in the format:
# /tmp/tmprandom/query_result_XXXX.json
# Let's try to construct it or find it

import os
import glob

# Look for the most recent query result file
files = glob.glob("/tmp/**/query_result_*.json", recursive=True)
books_file = None
for f in files:
    if 'books_info' in f or 'books_database' in f:
        books_file = f
        break

if not books_file:
    # Try a different approach - use the last file
    json_files = [f for f in glob.glob("/tmp/**/*.json", recursive=True) if 'query_result' in f]
    if json_files:
        books_file = sorted(json_files)[-1]

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({'books_file': books_file, 'files_found': files[:5]}))

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:6.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. var_functions.query_db:22.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:22.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

args.query:
SELECT 
    book_id,
    details,
    REGEXP_REPLACE(
        REGEXP_REPLACE(details, '.*(19\d{2}|20\d{2}).*', '\1'),
        '\D',
        '',
        'g'
    ) as publication_year
FROM books_info 
LIMIT 5;

exec_args.sql:
SELECT 
    book_id,

args.query:
WITH extracted_years AS (
    SELECT 
        book_id,
        CASE 
            WHEN details ~ '.*(19\\d{2}|20\\d{2}).*' THEN 
                CAST(SUBSTRING(details FROM '(19\\d{2}|20\\d{2})') AS INTEGER)
            ELSE NULL
        END as publication_year
    FROM books_info
)
SELECT 
    book_id,
    publication_year,
    FLOOR(publication_year / 10) * 10 as decade_start
FROM extracted_years
WHERE publication_year IS NOT NULL
ORDER BY publication_year;

            ELSE NULL
        END as publication_year
    FROM books_info
)
SELECT 
    book_id,
    publication_year,
    FLOOR(publication_year / 10) * 10 as decade_start
FROM extracted_years
WHERE publication_year IS NOT NULL
ORDER BY publication_year;

args.code:
import json
import re
import glob
import os

# Find the books_info query result file
books_files = glob.glob("/tmp/**/*.json", recursive=True)
books_file = None
for f in books_files:
    if ('book' in f.lower() or 'info' in f.lower()) and 'query_result' in f:
        books_file = f
        break

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({'books_file': books_file, 'all_files': books_files[:10]}))

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:6.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:22.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:22.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

args.query:
SELECT 
    book_id,
    CAST(SUBSTRING(details FROM '[0-9]{4}') AS INTEGER) as publication_year,
    (FLOOR(CAST(SUBSTRING(details FROM '[0-9]{4}') AS INTEGER) / 10) * 10) || 's' as decade
FROM books_info 
WHERE details ~ '[0-9]{4}' AND CAST(SUBSTRING(details FROM '[0-9]{4}') AS INTEGER) BETWEEN 1900 AND 2023;

    book_id,

    (FLOOR(CAST(SUBSTRING(details FROM '[0-9]{4}') AS INTEGER) / 10) * 10) || 's' as decade
FROM books_info 
WHERE details ~ '[0-9]{4}' AND CAST(SUBSTRING(details FROM '[0-9]{4}') AS INTEGER) BETWEEN 1900 AND 2023;

args.code:
import json
import re
import os
import glob

# Find the query result files
files = glob.glob("/tmp/**/query_result_*.json", recursive=True)

books_file = None
reviews_file = None

for f in files:
    try:
        with open(f, 'r') as file:
            data = json.load(file)
            if data and len(data) > 0:
                # Check if it has the structure of books data
                if 'book_id' in str(data[0]) and 'publication_year' in str(data[0]):
                    books_file = f
                    print(f"Found books file: {f}")
                # Check if it has the structure of reviews data  
                elif 'purchase_id' in str(data[0]) and 'rating' in str(data[0]):
                    reviews_file = f
                    print(f"Found reviews file: {f}")
    except:
        continue

# If we didn't find them, use the storage variables directly
if not books_file or not reviews_file:
    print("Looking for files in different way...")
    # The books data is in var_functions.query_db:52
    # The reviews data is in var_functions.query_db:28
    
    # Let's try to access them through the file system pattern
    all_json_files = glob.glob("/tmp/**/*.json", recursive=True)
    for f in all_json_files:
        try:
            with open(f, 'r') as file:
                data = json.load(file)
                if len(data) > 0:
                    print(f"File {f} has keys: {list(data[0].keys()) if isinstance(data[0], dict) else 'not dict'}")
        except:
            continue

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({'books_file': books_file, 'reviews_file': reviews_file, 'total_files': len(files)}))

var_functions.query_db:6.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. var_functions.query_db:22.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:22.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

val_args.code:
import json
import re
import os
import glob

# Find the query result files
files = glob.glob("/tmp/**/query_result_*.json", recursive=True)

books_file = None
reviews_file = None

for f in files:
    try:
        with open(f, 'r') as file:
            data = json.load(file)
            if data and len(data) > 0:
                # Check if it has the structure of books data
                if 'book_id' in str(data[0]) and 'publication_year' in str(data[0]):
                    books_file = f
                    print(f"Found books file: {f}")
                # Check if it has the structure of reviews data  
                elif 'purchase_id' in str(data[0]) and 'rating' in str(data[0]):
                    reviews_file = f
                    print(f"Found reviews file: {f}")
    except:
        continue

# If we didn't find them, use the storage variables directly
if not books_file or not reviews_file:
    print("Looking for files in different way...")
    # The books data is in var_functions.query_db:52
    # The reviews data is in var_functions.query_db:28
    
    # Let's try to access them through the file system pattern
    all_json_files = glob.glob("/tmp/**/*.json", recursive=True)
    for f in all_json_files:
        try:
            with open(f, 'r') as file:
                data = json.load(file)
                if len(data) > 0:
                    print(f"File {f} has keys: {list(data[0].keys()) if isinstance(data[0], dict) else 'not dict'}")
        except:
            continue

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({'books_file': books_file, 'reviews_file': reviews_file, 'total_files': len(files)}))

args.code:
import json
import os
import glob

# First, let's find the actual file paths for our data
base_dir = "/tmp"
all_files = []

# Walk through the directory tree to find our files
for root, dirs, files in os.walk(base_dir):
    for file in files:
        if file.endswith('.json') and 'query_result' in file:
            full_path = os.path.join(root, file)
            all_files.append(full_path)

# Let's examine the files to identify which is which
books_file = None
reviews_file = None

for f in all_files:
    try:
        with open(f, 'r') as file:
            data = json.load(file)
            if data and len(data) > 0:
                first_item = data[0]
                if isinstance(first_item, dict):
                    if 'book_id' in first_item and 'publication_year' in first_item:
                        books_file = f
                        print(f"Books file found: {f}")
                        print(f"Sample: {first_item}")
                    elif 'purchase_id' in first_item and 'rating' in first_item:
                        reviews_file = f
                        print(f"Reviews file found: {f}")
                        print(f"Sample: {first_item}")
    except Exception as e:
        continue

print(f"Total files found: {len(all_files)}")
print(f"Books file: {books_file}")
print(f"Reviews file: {reviews_file}")

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:6.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:22.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:22.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:6.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:22.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:22.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

args.code:
import json
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import os

# Read the books data with publication years
books_file_path = None
reviews_file_path = None

# Look for the files in the expected format
import glob
files = glob.glob('/tmp/**/query_result_*.json', recursive=True)

for f in files:
    try:
        with open(f, 'r') as file:
            data = json.load(file)
            if data and len(data) > 0:
                # Check structure to identify which file is which
                first_keys = list(data[0].keys())
                if 'book_id' in first_keys and 'publication_year' in first_keys:
                    books_file_path = f
                elif 'purchase_id' in first_keys and 'rating' in first_keys:
                    reviews_file_path = f
    except:
        continue

print(f"Books file: {books_file_path}")
print(f"Reviews file: {reviews_file_path}")

if not books_file_path or not reviews_file_path:
    print("Could not find one or both files")
    # Try to load from the storage variables if possible
    import sys
    try:
        # This is a fallback - in real execution, the variables would be available
        pass
    except:
        print("Could not load data")
        exit

# Load books data
with open(books_file_path, 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

# Load reviews data  
with open(reviews_file_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews_data = json.load(f)

# Create DataFrames
df_books = pd.DataFrame(books_data)
df_reviews = pd.DataFrame(reviews_data)

# Convert data types
df_books['publication_year'] = pd.to_numeric(df_books['publication_year'])
df_reviews['rating'] = pd.to_numeric(df_reviews['rating'])

# Extract decade from publication year
df_books['decade'] = (df_books['publication_year'] // 10) * 10
df_books['decade_label'] = df_books['decade'].astype(str) + 's'

# Merge books with reviews
# Note: purchase_id in reviews corresponds to book_id in books
merged = df_reviews.merge(
    df_books[['book_id', 'decade', 'decade_label']], 
    left_on='purchase_id', 
    right_on='book_id', 
    how='inner'
)

# Group by decade and calculate statistics
decade_stats = merged.groupby('decade_label').agg(
    num_books=('purchase_id', 'nunique'),
    avg_rating=('rating', 'mean'),
    total_reviews=('rating', 'count')
).reset_index()

# Filter decades with at least 10 distinct books
decade_stats_filtered = decade_stats[decade_stats['num_books'] >= 10]

# Sort by average rating descending
decade_stats_sorted = decade_stats_filtered.sort_values('avg_rating', ascending=False)

print(f"Total books with publication years: {len(df_books)}")
print(f"Total reviews: {len(df_reviews)}")
print(f"Books with reviews after merge: {merged['purchase_id'].nunique()}")
print(f"Decades meeting the 10-book threshold: {len(decade_stats_filtered)}")
print("\nTop decades by average rating:")
print(decade_stats_sorted.head(10).to_string(index=False))

# Get the decade with highest average rating
top_decade = decade_stats_sorted.iloc[0]

print(f"\nTop decade: {top_decade['decade_label']} with avg rating {top_decade['avg_rating']:.2f} from {top_decade['num_books']} books")

# Save the results for the final answer
result = {
    'top_decade': top_decade['decade_label'],
    'avg_rating': float(top_decade['avg_rating']),
    'num_books': int(top_decade['num_books']),
    'all_decades': decade_stats_sorted.to_dict('records')
}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:6.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:22.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:22.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

val_args.code:
import json
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import os

# Read the books data with publication years
books_file_path = None
reviews_file_path = None

# Look for the files in the expected format
import glob
files = glob.glob('/tmp/**/query_result_*.json', recursive=True)

for f in files:
    try:
        with open(f, 'r') as file:
            data = json.load(file)
            if data and len(data) > 0:
                # Check structure to identify which file is which
                first_keys = list(data[0].keys())
                if 'book_id' in first_keys and 'publication_year' in first_keys:
                    books_file_path = f
                elif 'purchase_id' in first_keys and 'rating' in first_keys:
                    reviews_file_path = f
    except:
        continue

print(f"Books file: {books_file_path}")
print(f"Reviews file: {reviews_file_path}")

if not books_file_path or not reviews_file_path:
    print("Could not find one or both files")
    # Try to load from the storage variables if possible
    import sys
    try:
        # This is a fallback - in real execution, the variables would be available
        pass
    except:
        print("Could not load data")
        exit

# Load books data
with open(books_file_path, 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

# Load reviews data  
with open(reviews_file_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews_data = json.load(f)

# Create DataFrames
df_books = pd.DataFrame(books_data)
df_reviews = pd.DataFrame(reviews_data)

# Convert data types
df_books['publication_year'] = pd.to_numeric(df_books['publication_year'])
df_reviews['rating'] = pd.to_numeric(df_reviews['rating'])

# Extract decade from publication year
df_books['decade'] = (df_books['publication_year'] // 10) * 10
df_books['decade_label'] = df_books['decade'].astype(str) + 's'

# Merge books with reviews
# Note: purchase_id in reviews corresponds to book_id in books
merged = df_reviews.merge(
    df_books[['book_id', 'decade', 'decade_label']], 
    left_on='purchase_id', 
    right_on='book_id', 
    how='inner'
)

# Group by decade and calculate statistics
decade_stats = merged.groupby('decade_label').agg(
    num_books=('purchase_id', 'nunique'),
    avg_rating=('rating', 'mean'),
    total_reviews=('rating', 'count')
).reset_index()

# Filter decades with at least 10 distinct books
decade_stats_filtered = decade_stats[decade_stats['num_books'] >= 10]

# Sort by average rating descending
decade_stats_sorted = decade_stats_filtered.sort_values('avg_rating', ascending=False)

print(f"Total books with publication years: {len(df_books)}")
print(f"Total reviews: {len(df_reviews)}")
print(f"Books with reviews after merge: {merged['purchase_id'].nunique()}")
print(f"Decades meeting the 10-book threshold: {len(decade_stats_filtered)}")
print("\nTop decades by average rating:")
print(decade_stats_sorted.head(10).to_string(index=False))

# Get the decade with highest average rating
top_decade = decade_stats_sorted.iloc[0]

print(f"\nTop decade: {top_decade['decade_label']} with avg rating {top_decade['avg_rating']:.2f} from {top_decade['num_books']} books")

# Save the results for the final answer
result = {
    'top_decade': top_decade['decade_label'],
    'avg_rating': float(top_decade['avg_rating']),
    'num_books': int(top_decade['num_books']),
    'all_decades': decade_stats_sorted.to_dict('records')
}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:6.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:22.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:22.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

val_args.code:
import json
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import glob

# Find the query result files
books_file_path = None
reviews_file_path = None
files = glob.glob('/tmp/**/query_result_*.json', recursive=True)

for f in files:
    try:
        with open(f, 'r') as file:
            data = json.load(file)
            if data and len(data) > 0:
                first_keys = list(data[0].keys())
                if 'book_id' in first_keys and 'publication_year' in first_keys:
                    books_file_path = f
                elif 'purchase_id' in first_keys and 'rating' in first_keys:
                    reviews_file_path = f
    except:
        continue

# Load books data
with open(books_file_path, 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

# Load reviews data  
with open(reviews_file_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews_data = json.load(f)

# Create DataFrames
df_books = pd.DataFrame(books_data)
df_reviews = pd.DataFrame(reviews_data)

# Convert data types
df_books['publication_year'] = pd.to_numeric(df_books['publication_year'])
df_reviews['rating'] = pd.to_numeric(df_reviews['rating'])

# Extract decade from publication year
df_books['decade'] = (df_books['publication_year'] // 10) * 10
df_books['decade_label'] = df_books['decade'].astype(str) + 's'

# Merge books with reviews
merged = df_reviews.merge(
    df_books[['book_id', 'decade', 'decade_label']], 
    left_on='purchase_id', 
    right_on='book_id', 
    how='inner'
)

# Group by decade and calculate statistics
decade_stats = merged.groupby('decade_label').agg(
    num_books=('purchase_id', 'nunique'),
    avg_rating=('rating', 'mean'),
    total_reviews=('rating', 'count')
).reset_index()

# Filter decades with at least 10 distinct books
decade_stats_filtered = decade_stats[decade_stats['num_books'] >= 10]

# Sort by average rating descending
decade_stats_sorted = decade_stats_filtered.sort_values('avg_rating', ascending=False)

# Get the decade with highest average rating
top_decade = decade_stats_sorted.iloc[0]

# Save the results
result = {
    'top_decade': top_decade['decade_label'],
    'avg_rating': float(top_decade['avg_rating']),
    'num_books': int(top_decade['num_books']),
    'total_reviews': int(top_decade['total_reviews']),
    'all_decades': decade_stats_sorted.to_dict('records')
}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:6.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:22.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:22.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

args.code:
import json
import pandas as pd
import glob
import os

print('Looking for files...')

# Let's check what's in the current working directory and /tmp
for directory in ['.', '/tmp']:
    print(f'\nDirectory: {directory}')
    if os.path.exists(directory):
        files = os.listdir(directory)
        json_files = [f for f in files if f.endswith('.json')]
        print(f'JSON files: {json_files[:10]}')
        
        # Check subdirectories
        for item in files:
            full_path = os.path.join(directory, item)
            if os.path.isdir(full_path):
                subfiles = os.listdir(full_path)
                sub_json = [f for f in subfiles if f.endswith('.json')]
                if sub_json:
                    print(f'  {item}/: {sub_json[:5]}')

# Try different patterns to find our files
patterns = [
    '/tmp/**/*.json',
    '/tmp/*.json',
    './**/query_result_*.json',
    './*.json'
]

all_json_files = []
for pattern in patterns:
    all_json_files.extend(glob.glob(pattern, recursive=True))

print(f'\nAll JSON files found: {len(all_json_files)}')
for f in all_json_files[:10]:
    print(f'  {f}')

# Try to access the variable directly - it should be loaded in the environment
import sys
print('\nChecking available variables...')
print('Current Python environment has access to:')
for name in dir():
    if 'query_db' in name and 'var_' not in name:
        print(f'  {name}')

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({'files_found': len(all_json_files), 'sample_files': all_json_files[:5]}))

import json
import pandas as pd
import glob
import os

print('Looking for files...')

# Let's check what's in the current working directory and /tmp
for directory in ['.', '/tmp']:
    print(f'\nDirectory: {directory}')
    if os.path.exists(directory):
        files = os.listdir(directory)
        json_files = [f for f in files if f.endswith('.json')]
        print(f'JSON files: {json_files[:10]}')
        
        # Check subdirectories
        for item in files:
            full_path = os.path.join(directory, item)
            if os.path.isdir(full_path):
                subfiles = os.listdir(full_path)
                sub_json = [f for f in subfiles if f.endswith('.json')]
                if sub_json:
                    print(f'  {item}/: {sub_json[:5]}')

# Try different patterns to find our files
patterns = [
    '/tmp/**/*.json',
    '/tmp/*.json',
    './**/query_result_*.json',
    './*.json'
]

all_json_files = []
for pattern in patterns:
    all_json_files.extend(glob.glob(pattern, recursive=True))

print(f'\nAll JSON files found: {len(all_json_files)}')
for f in all_json_files[:10]:
    print(f'  {f}')

# Try to access the variable directly - it should be loaded in the environment
import sys
print('\nChecking available variables...')
print('Current Python environment has access to:')
for name in dir():
    if 'query_db' in name and 'var_' not in name:
        print(f'  {name}')

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({'files_found': len(all_json_files), 'sample_files': all_json_files[:5]}))

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:6.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.
