var_functions.query_db:22.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:22.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

var_functions.query_db:22.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:22.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:22.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

args.code:
import json
import pandas as pd

# Since we can't access the variables directly due to naming issues,
# let's re-query the data using the tool results that were already fetched

# The books data with publication years was stored in var_functions.query_db:64
# The reviews data was stored in var_functions.query_db:28
# Let's load them from the files they were stored in

import os
import glob

# Find the most recent query result files
files = sorted(glob.glob('/tmp/**/query_result_*.json', recursive=True), key=os.path.getmtime, reverse=True)

books_file = None
reviews_file = None

for f in files:
    try:
        with open(f, 'r') as file:
            data = json.load(file)
            if data and len(data) > 0:
                first_keys = list(data[0].keys())
                if 'book_id' in first_keys and 'publication_year' in first_keys:
                    books_file = f
                elif 'purchase_id' in first_keys and 'rating' in first_keys:
                    reviews_file = f
    except:
        continue

if books_file and reviews_file:
    # Load the data
    with open(books_file, 'r') as f:
        books_data = json.load(f)
    
    with open(reviews_file, 'r') as f:
        reviews_data = json.load(f)
    
    # Create DataFrames
    df_books = pd.DataFrame(books_data)
    df_reviews = pd.DataFrame(reviews_data)
    
    # Convert data types
    df_books['publication_year'] = pd.to_numeric(df_books['publication_year'])
    df_reviews['rating'] = pd.to_numeric(df_reviews['rating'])
    
    # Extract decade
    df_books['decade'] = (df_books['publication_year'] // 10) * 10
    df_books['decade_label'] = df_books['decade'].astype(str) + 's'
    
    # Merge with reviews
    merged = df_reviews.merge(
        df_books[['book_id', 'decade', 'decade_label']], 
        left_on='purchase_id', 
        right_on='book_id', 
        how='inner'
    )
    
    # Calculate decade statistics
    decade_stats = merged.groupby('decade_label').agg(
        num_books=('purchase_id', 'nunique'),
        avg_rating=('rating', 'mean'),
        total_reviews=('rating', 'count')
    ).reset_index()
    
    # Filter for decades with at least 10 books
    decade_stats_filtered = decade_stats[decade_stats['num_books'] >= 10]
    
    # Sort by average rating
    decade_stats_sorted = decade_stats_filtered.sort_values('avg_rating', ascending=False)
    
    # Get top decade
    top_decade = decade_stats_sorted.iloc[0]
    
    result = {
        'top_decade': top_decade['decade_label'],
        'avg_rating': float(top_decade['avg_rating']),
        'num_books': int(top_decade['num_books']),
        'total_reviews': int(top_decade['total_reviews'])
    }
else:
    result = {
        'error': 'Could not find required data files',
        'files_found': len(files),
        'books_file': books_file,
        'reviews_file': reviews_file
    }

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:6.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:22.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:22.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:22.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:22.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:22.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:22.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

var_functions.query_db:22.text: Looked online for years for something like this.  It's the best I've seen.

args.query: SELECT book_id, publication_year, FLOOR(publication_year / 10) * 10 as decade FROM (SELECT book_id, CAST(SUBSTRING(details FROM '[0-9]{4}') AS INTEGER) as publication_year FROM books_info WHERE details ~ '[0-9]{4}' AND CAST(SUBSTRING(details FROM '[0-9]{4}') AS INTEGER) BETWEEN 1900 AND 2023) sub;

exec_args.sql: SELECT book_id, publication_year, FLOOR(publication_year / 10) * 10 as decade FROM (SELECT book_id, CAST(SUBSTRING(details FROM '[0-9]{4}') AS INTEGER) as publication_year FROM books_info WHERE details ~ '[0-9]{4}' AND CAST(SUBSTRING(details FROM '[0-9]{4}') AS INTEGER) BETWEEN 1900 AND 2023) sub;

args.code:
import json
import pandas as pd

# Load the data
books_data = var_functions.query_db_80
reviews_data = var_functions.query_db_76

# Create DataFrames
df_books = pd.DataFrame(books_data)
df_reviews = pd.DataFrame(reviews_data)

# Clean data types
df_books['publication_year'] = df_books['publication_year'].astype(int)
df_books['decade'] = df_books['decade'].astype(int)
df_reviews['rating'] = df_reviews['rating'].astype(float)

# Create decade labels
df_books['decade_label'] = df_books['decade'].astype(str) + 's'

# Merge books with reviews
merged = df_reviews.merge(
    df_books[['book_id', 'publication_year', 'decade', 'decade_label']], 
    left_on='purchase_id', 
    right_on='book_id', 
    how='inner'
)

# Group by decade and calculate statistics
decade_stats = merged.groupby('decade_label').agg(
    num_books=('purchase_id', 'nunique'),
    avg_rating=('rating', 'mean')
).reset_index()

# Filter for decades with at least 10 books
decade_stats_filtered = decade_stats[decade_stats['num_books'] >= 10]

# Sort by average rating descending
decade_stats_sorted = decade_stats_filtered.sort_values('avg_rating', ascending=False)

# Get top decade
top_decade = decade_stats_sorted.iloc[0]

result = {
    'top_decade': top_decade['decade_label'],
    'avg_rating': float(top_decade['avg_rating']),
    'num_books': int(top_decade['num_books'])
}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:6.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:22.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:22.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:22.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:22.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

val_args.code:
import json
import pandas as pd

# Load the data
books_data = var_functions.query_db_80
reviews_data = var_functions.query_db_76

# Create DataFrames
df_books = pd.DataFrame(books_data)
df_reviews = pd.DataFrame(reviews_data)

# Clean data types
df_books['publication_year'] = df_books['publication_year'].astype(int)
df_books['decade'] = df_books['decade'].astype(int)
df_reviews['rating'] = df_reviews['rating'].astype(float)

# Create decade labels
df_books['decade_label'] = df_books['decade'].astype(str) + 's'

# Merge books with reviews
merged = df_reviews.merge(
    df_books[['book_id', 'publication_year', 'decade', 'decade_label']], 
    left_on='purchase_id', 
    right_on='book_id', 
    how='inner'
)

# Group by decade and calculate statistics
decade_stats = merged.groupby('decade_label').agg(
    num_books=('purchase_id', 'nunique'),
    avg_rating=('rating', 'mean')
).reset_index()

# Filter for decades with at least 10 books
decade_stats_filtered = decade_stats[decade_stats['num_books'] >= 10]

# Sort by average rating descending
decade_stats_sorted = decade_stats_filtered.sort_values('avg_rating', ascending=False)

# Get top decade
top_decade = decade_stats_sorted.iloc[0]

result = {
    'top_decade': top_decade['decade_label'],
    'avg_rating': float(top_decade['avg_rating']),
    'num_books': int(top_decade['num_books'])
}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))

args.code:
import json
import pandas as pd
import os

# Try to find the actual files from the temp directory
tmp_dir = "/tmp"
all_files = []

# Walk through /tmp to find our query result files
for root, dirs, files in os.walk(tmp_dir):
    for file in files:
        if file.endswith('.json') and 'query_result' in file:
            full_path = os.path.join(root, file)
            all_files.append(full_path)

# Identify which file is which
def identify_file(filepath):
    try:
        with open(filepath, 'r') as f:
            data = json.load(f)
            if data and len(data) > 0:
                first_item = data[0]
                if isinstance(first_item, dict):
                    keys = list(first_item.keys())
                    if 'book_id' in keys and 'decade' in keys:
                        return 'books'
                    elif 'purchase_id' in keys and 'rating' in keys:
                        return 'reviews'
    except:
        pass
    return None

# Find our files
books_file = None
reviews_file = None

for f in all_files:
    file_type = identify_file(f)
    if file_type == 'books':
        books_file = f
    elif file_type == 'reviews':
        reviews_file = f

if books_file and reviews_file:
    # Load and process the data
    with open(books_file, 'r') as f:
        books_data = json.load(f)
    
    with open(reviews_file, 'r') as f:
        reviews_data = json.load(f)
    
    # Create DataFrames
    df_books = pd.DataFrame(books_data)
    df_reviews = pd.DataFrame(reviews_data)
    
    # Convert data types
    df_books['publication_year'] = df_books['publication_year'].astype(int)
    df_books['decade'] = df_books['decade'].astype(int)
    df_reviews['rating'] = df_reviews['rating'].astype(float)
    
    # Create decade labels
    df_books['decade_label'] = df_books['decade'].astype(str) + 's'
    
    # Merge books with reviews
    merged = df_reviews.merge(
        df_books[['book_id', 'decade', 'decade_label']], 
        left_on='purchase_id', 
        right_on='book_id', 
        how='inner'
    )
    
    # Group by decade
    decade_stats = merged.groupby('decade_label').agg(
        num_books=('purchase_id', 'nunique'),
        avg_rating=('rating', 'mean')
    ).reset_index()
    
    # Filter for decades with at least 10 books and sort
    decade_stats_filtered = decade_stats[decade_stats['num_books'] >= 10]
    decade_stats_sorted = decade_stats_filtered.sort_values('avg_rating', ascending=False)
    
    # Get top decade
    top_decade = decade_stats_sorted.iloc[0]
    
    result = {
        'top_decade': top_decade['decade_label'],
        'avg_rating': float(top_decade['avg_rating']),
        'num_books': int(top_decade['num_books']),
        'all_decades': decade_stats_sorted.to_dict('records')
    }
else:
    result = {
        'error': 'Could not find both required files',
        'books_file': books_file,
        'reviews_file': reviews_file,
        'files_found': all_files[:5]
    }

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

val_args.code:
import json
import pandas as pd
import os

# Try to find the actual files from the temp directory
tmp_dir = "/tmp"
all_files = []

# Walk through /tmp to find our query result files
for root, dirs, files in os.walk(tmp_dir):
    for file in files:
        if file.endswith('.json') and 'query_result' in file:
            full_path = os.path.join(root, file)
            all_files.append(full_path)

# Identify which file is which
def identify_file(filepath):
    try:
        with open(filepath, 'r') as f:
            data = json.load(f)
            if data and len(data) > 0:
                first_item = data[0]
                if isinstance(first_item, dict):
                    keys = list(first_item.keys())
                    if 'book_id' in keys and 'decade' in keys:
                        return 'books'
                    elif 'purchase_id' in keys and 'rating' in keys:
                        return 'reviews'
    except:
        pass
    return None

# Find our files
books_file = None
reviews_file = None

for f in all_files:
    file_type = identify_file(f)
    if file_type == 'books':
        books_file = f
    elif file_type == 'reviews':
        reviews_file = f

if books_file and reviews_file:
    # Load and process the data
    with open(books_file, 'r') as f:
        books_data = json.load(f)
    
    with open(reviews_file, 'r') as f:
        reviews_data = json.load(f)
    
    # Create DataFrames
    df_books = pd.DataFrame(books_data)
    df_reviews = pd.DataFrame(reviews_data)
    
    # Convert data types
    df_books['publication_year'] = df_books['publication_year'].astype(int)
    df_books['decade'] = df_books['decade'].astype(int)
    df_reviews['rating'] = df_reviews['rating'].astype(float)
    
    # Create decade labels
    df_books['decade_label'] = df_books['decade'].astype(str) + 's'
    
    # Merge books with reviews
    merged = df_reviews.merge(
        df_books[['book_id', 'decade', 'decade_label']], 
        left_on='purchase_id', 
        right_on='book_id', 
        how='inner'
    )
    
    # Group by decade
    decade_stats = merged.groupby('decade_label').agg(
        num_books=('purchase_id', 'nunique'),
        avg_rating=('rating', 'mean')
    ).reset_index()
    
    # Filter for decades with at least 10 books and sort
    decade_stats_filtered = decade_stats[decade_stats['num_books'] >= 10]
    decade_stats_sorted = decade_stats_filtered.sort_values('avg_rating', ascending=False)
    
    # Get top decade
    top_decade = decade_stats_sorted.iloc[0]
    
    result = {
        'top_decade': top_decade['decade_label'],
        'avg_rating': float(top_decade['avg_rating']),
        'num_books': int(top_decade['num_books']),
        'all_decades': decade_stats_sorted.to_dict('records')
    }
else:
    result = {
        'error': 'Could not find both required files',
        'books_file': books_file,
        'reviews_file': reviews_file,
        'files_found': all_files[:5]
    }

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

val_args.code:
import json
import pandas as pd
import os
import re

# The query results are stored in the current environment as variables
# Let's find them by checking what variables are available

# Get all variables from the environment
all_vars = dir()

# Find the books data (should have decade or publication_year)
books_data = None
reviews_data = None

for var_name in all_vars:
    if 'query_db' in var_name and ':' in var_name:
        # This is likely one of our result variables
        var_value = globals()[var_name]
        if isinstance(var_value, str) and os.path.exists(var_value) and var_value.endswith('.json'):
            # This is a file path to query results
            try:
                with open(var_value, 'r') as f:
                    data = json.load(f)
                    if data and len(data) > 0:
                        keys = list(data[0].keys())
                        if 'book_id' in keys and 'decade' in keys:
                            books_data = data
                            print(f'Found books data in {var_name}')
                        elif 'purchase_id' in keys and 'rating' in keys:
                            reviews_data = data
                            print(f'Found reviews data in {var_name}')
            except:
                continue

# Check if we found the data
if not books_data or not reviews_data:
    # Try alternative approach - look for common patterns
    for filename in ['/tmp/query_result.json', '/tmp/tmpxxx/query_result.json']:
        try:
            with open(filename, 'r') as f:
                data = json.load(f)
                if data:
                    keys = list(data[0].keys())
                    if 'book_id' in keys and 'decade' in keys:
                        books_data = data
                    elif 'purchase_id' in keys and 'rating' in keys:
                        reviews_data = data
        except:
            pass

if not books_data or not reviews_data:
    print('Could not load data from files')
    # Generate a test result to debug
    result = {'error': 'Data loading failed', 'books_found': books_data is not None, 'reviews_found': reviews_data is not None}
else:
    # Process the data
    df_books = pd.DataFrame(books_data)
    df_reviews = pd.DataFrame(reviews_data)
    
    # Convert data types
    df_books['decade'] = df_books['decade'].astype(int)
    df_reviews['rating'] = df_reviews['rating'].astype(float)
    
    # Create decade labels
    df_books['decade_label'] = df_books['decade'].astype(str) + 's'
    
    # Merge data
    merged = df_reviews.merge(
        df_books[['book_id', 'decade', 'decade_label']], 
        left_on='purchase_id', 
        right_on='book_id', 
        how='inner'
    )
    
    # Group by decade
    decade_stats = merged.groupby('decade_label').agg(
        num_books=('purchase_id', 'nunique'),
        avg_rating=('rating', 'mean')
    ).reset_index()
    
    # Filter and sort
    decade_stats_filtered = decade_stats[decade_stats['num_books'] >= 10]
    decade_stats_sorted = decade_stats_filtered.sort_values('avg_rating', ascending=False)
    
    # Get result
    if len(decade_stats_sorted) > 0:
        top_decade = decade_stats_sorted.iloc[0]
        result = {
            'top_decade': top_decade['decade_label'],
            'avg_rating': float(top_decade['avg_rating']),
            'num_books': int(top_decade['num_books'])
        }
    else:
        result = {'error': 'No decades with 10+ books found'}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:6.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

args.query:
SELECT 
    book_id, 
    CAST(SUBSTRING(details FROM '[0-9]{4}') AS INTEGER) as publication_year,
    CAST((CAST(SUBSTRING(details FROM '[0-9]{4}') AS INTEGER) / 10) * 10 AS INTEGER) as decade
FROM books_info 
WHERE details ~ '[0-9]{4}' AND CAST(SUBSTRING(details FROM '[0-9]{4}') AS INTEGER) BETWEEN 1900 AND 2023;

SELECT 
    book_id, 
    CAST(SUBSTRING(details FROM '[0-9]{4}') AS INTEGER) as publication_year,

FROM books_info 
WHERE details ~ '[0-9]{4}' AND CAST(SUBSTRING(details FROM '[0-9]{4}') AS INTEGER) BETWEEN 1900 AND 2023;

args.code:
import json
import pandas as pd
import os

# Load books data and reviews data
books_file = var_functions.query_db_88
reviews_file = var_functions.query_db_76

with open(books_file, 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

with open(reviews_file, 'r') as f:
    reviews_data = json.load(f)

# Create DataFrames
df_books = pd.DataFrame(books_data)
df_reviews = pd.DataFrame(reviews_data)

# Convert data types
df_books['decade'] = df_books['decade'].astype(int)
df_reviews['rating'] = df_reviews['rating'].astype(float)

# Create decade labels
df_books['decade_label'] = df_books['decade'].astype(str) + 's'

# Merge books with reviews
merged = df_reviews.merge(
    df_books[['book_id', 'decade', 'decade_label']], 
    left_on='purchase_id', 
    right_on='book_id', 
    how='inner'
)

# Group by decade and calculate statistics
decade_stats = merged.groupby('decade_label').agg(
    num_books=('purchase_id', 'nunique'),
    avg_rating=('rating', 'mean')
).reset_index()

# Filter for decades with at least 10 distinct books
decade_stats_filtered = decade_stats[decade_stats['num_books'] >= 10]

# Sort by average rating descending
decade_stats_sorted = decade_stats_filtered.sort_values('avg_rating', ascending=False)

# Get the top decade
top_decade = decade_stats_sorted.iloc[0]

result = {
    'top_decade': top_decade['decade_label'],
    'avg_rating': round(float(top_decade['avg_rating']), 2),
    'num_books': int(top_decade['num_books'])
}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:6.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:22.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:22.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

args.code:
import json
import pandas as pd
import os
import glob

# Find all JSON result files in /tmp and subdirectories
result_files = glob.glob('/tmp/**/query_result_*.json', recursive=True)

books_data = None
reviews_data = None
books_file_path = None
reviews_file_path = None

# Identify which file contains books data and which contains reviews
for file_path in result_files:
    try:
        with open(file_path, 'r') as f:
            data = json.load(f)
            if data and len(data) > 0:
                first_item = data[0]
                if isinstance(first_item, dict):
                    keys = list(first_item.keys())
                    if 'decade' in keys and 'book_id' in keys:
                        books_data = data
                        books_file_path = file_path
                    elif 'purchase_id' in keys and 'rating' in keys:
                        reviews_data = data
                        reviews_file_path = file_path
    except Exception as e:
        continue

# Check if we found both datasets
if not books_data or not reviews_data:
    result = {
        'error': 'Could not find required data files',
        'books_found': books_data is not None,
        'reviews_found': reviews_data is not None,
        'files_checked': len(result_files)
    }
else:
    # Process the data
    df_books = pd.DataFrame(books_data)
    df_reviews = pd.DataFrame(reviews_data)
    
    # Convert data types with error handling
    df_books['decade'] = pd.to_numeric(df_books['decade'], errors='coerce').astype('Int64')
    df_reviews['rating'] = pd.to_numeric(df_reviews['rating'], errors='coerce')
    
    # Create decade labels
    df_books['decade_label'] = df_books['decade'].astype(str) + 's'
    
    # Merge datasets - purchase_id in reviews corresponds to book_id in books
    merged = df_reviews.merge(
        df_books[['book_id', 'decade', 'decade_label']], 
        left_on='purchase_id', 
        right_on='book_id', 
        how='inner'
    )
    
    # Group by decade and calculate statistics
    decade_stats = merged.groupby('decade_label').agg(
        num_books=('purchase_id', 'nunique'),
        avg_rating=('rating', 'mean')
    ).reset_index()
    
    # Filter for decades with at least 10 distinct books
    decade_stats_filtered = decade_stats[decade_stats['num_books'] >= 10]
    
    # Sort by average rating (descending)
    decade_stats_sorted = decade_stats_filtered.sort_values('avg_rating', ascending=False)
    
    if len(decade_stats_sorted) > 0:
        top_decade = decade_stats_sorted.iloc[0]
        result = {
            'top_decade': top_decade['decade_label'],
            'avg_rating': round(float(top_decade['avg_rating']), 2),
            'num_books': int(top_decade['num_books'])
        }
    else:
        result = {
            'error': 'No decades found with at least 10 books rated',
            'total_decades': len(decade_stats),
            'books_matched': len(merged['purchase_id'].unique())
        }

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:6.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:22.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:22.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

import json
import pandas as pd

# Load both datasets directly using the tool results
# Books data with publication years and decades
books_data = var_functions.query_db_88

# Reviews data with ratings  
reviews_data = var_functions.query_db_76

# Convert to DataFrames
df_books = pd.DataFrame(books_data)
print(f"Books data shape: {df_books.shape}")
print(f"Books columns: {df_books.columns.tolist()}")
print(f"First few books: {df_books.head()}")

df_reviews = pd.DataFrame(reviews_data)
print(f"\nReviews data shape: {df_reviews.shape}")
print(f"Reviews columns: {df_reviews.columns.tolist()}")
print(f"First few reviews: {df_reviews.head()}")

# Save results for further processing
books_json = df_books.to_json(orient='records')
reviews_json = df_reviews.to_json(orient='records')

result = {
    'books_loaded': True,
    'books_shape': df_books.shape,
    'reviews_loaded': True, 
    'reviews_shape': df_reviews.shape
}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:6.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:22.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:22.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

args.code:
import json
import pandas as pd
import os
import glob

# We need to find the query result files from our previous queries
temp_dir = "/tmp"
result_files = glob.glob(os.path.join(temp_dir, "**/query_result_*.json"), recursive=True)

books_data = None
reviews_data = None

# Find which file is which
for filepath in result_files:
    try:
        with open(filepath, 'r') as f:
            data = json.load(f)
            if data and len(data) > 0:
                first_item = data[0]
                if isinstance(first_item, dict):
                    keys = first_item.keys()
                    # Books data has 'decade' and 'book_id'
                    if 'decade' in keys and 'book_id' in keys:
                        books_data = data
                    # Reviews data has 'purchase_id' and 'rating'
                    elif 'purchase_id' in keys and 'rating' in keys:
                        reviews_data = data
    except Exception as e:
        continue

# Verify we have both datasets
if not books_data or not reviews_data:
    result = {
        'error': 'Could not load both datasets',
        'books_found': books_data is not None,
        'reviews_found': reviews_data is not None,
        'files_found': len(result_files)
    }
else:
    # Create DataFrames
    df_books = pd.DataFrame(books_data)
    df_reviews = pd.DataFrame(reviews_data)
    
    # Convert data types
    df_books['decade'] = pd.to_numeric(df_books['decade'], errors='coerce').astype('Int64')
    df_reviews['rating'] = pd.to_numeric(df_reviews['rating'], errors='coerce')
    
    # Create decade labels
    df_books['decade_label'] = df_books['decade'].astype(str) + 's'
    
    # Merge books with reviews (purchase_id = book_id)
    merged = df_reviews.merge(
        df_books[['book_id', 'decade', 'decade_label']], 
        left_on='purchase_id', 
        right_on='book_id', 
        how='inner'
    )
    
    # Group by decade
    decade_stats = merged.groupby('decade_label').agg(
        num_books=('purchase_id', 'nunique'),
        avg_rating=('rating', 'mean')
    ).reset_index()
    
    # Filter for decades with at least 10 distinct books
    decade_stats_filtered = decade_stats[decade_stats['num_books'] >= 10]
    
    # Sort by average rating (descending)
    decade_stats_sorted = decade_stats_filtered.sort_values('avg_rating', ascending=False)
    
    # Get the top result
    if len(decade_stats_sorted) > 0:
        top_decade = decade_stats_sorted.iloc[0]
        result = {
            'top_decade': top_decade['decade_label'],
            'avg_rating': round(float(top_decade['avg_rating']), 2),
            'num_books': int(top_decade['num_books'])
        }
    else:
        result = {
            'error': 'No decades met the 10-book threshold',
            'total_decades': len(decade_stats),
            'books_analyzed': len(merged['purchase_id'].unique())
        }

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:6.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:22.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:22.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

args.query:
SELECT 
    book_id,
    CAST(SUBSTRING(details FROM '[0-9]{4}') AS INTEGER) as publication_year,
    CAST((CAST(SUBSTRING(details FROM '[0-9]{4}') AS INTEGER) / 10) * 10 AS INTEGER) as decade
FROM books_info 
WHERE details ~ '[0-9]{4}' 
    AND CAST(SUBSTRING(details FROM '[0-9]{4}') AS INTEGER) BETWEEN 1900 AND 2023;

args.code:
import json
import pandas as pd
import os

# Load the data
books_file = var_functions.query_db_104
reviews_file = var_functions.query_db_76

# Read the data
with open(books_file, 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

with open(reviews_file, 'r') as f:
    reviews_data = json.load(f)

# Create DataFrames
df_books = pd.DataFrame(books_data)
df_reviews = pd.DataFrame(reviews_data)

# Convert data types
df_books['decade'] = df_books['decade'].astype(int)
df_reviews['rating'] = df_reviews['rating'].astype(float)

# Create decade labels
df_books['decade_label'] = df_books['decade'].astype(str) + 's'

# Merge books with reviews (purchase_id = book_id)
merged = df_reviews.merge(
    df_books[['book_id', 'decade', 'decade_label']], 
    left_on='purchase_id', 
    right_on='book_id', 
    how='inner'
)

# Group by decade and calculate statistics
decade_stats = merged.groupby('decade_label').agg(
    num_books=('purchase_id', 'nunique'),
    avg_rating=('rating', 'mean')
).reset_index()

# Filter for decades with at least 10 distinct books
decade_stats_filtered = decade_stats[decade_stats['num_books'] >= 10]

# Sort by average rating descending
decade_stats_sorted = decade_stats_filtered.sort_values('avg_rating', ascending=False)

# Get the top decade
top_decade = decade_stats_sorted.iloc[0]

result = {
    'top_decade': top_decade['decade_label'],
    'avg_rating': round(float(top_decade['avg_rating']), 2),
    'num_books': int(top_decade['num_books']),
    'all_decades': decade_stats_sorted.to_dict('records')
}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result, indent=2))

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:6.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

var_functions.query_db:22.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:22.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:22.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:22.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

val_args.code:
import json
import pandas as pd
import os

# Load the data
books_file = var_functions.query_db_104
reviews_file = var_functions.query_db_76

# Read the data
with open(books_file, 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)

with open(reviews_file, 'r') as f:
    reviews_data = json.load(f)

# Create DataFrames
df_books = pd.DataFrame(books_data)
df_reviews = pd.DataFrame(reviews_data)

# Convert data types
df_books['decade'] = df_books['decade'].astype(int)
df_reviews['rating'] = df_reviews['rating'].astype(float)

# Create decade labels
df_books['decade_label'] = df_books['decade'].astype(str) + 's'

# Merge books with reviews (purchase_id = book_id)
merged = df_reviews.merge(
    df_books[['book_id', 'decade', 'decade_label']], 
    left_on='purchase_id', 
    right_on='book_id', 
    how='inner'
)

# Group by decade and calculate statistics
decade_stats = merged.groupby('decade_label').agg(
    num_books=('purchase_id', 'nunique'),
    avg_rating=('rating', 'mean')
).reset_index()

# Filter for decades with at least 10 distinct books
decade_stats_filtered = decade_stats[decade_stats['num_books'] >= 10]

# Sort by average rating descending
decade_stats_sorted = decade_stats_filtered.sort_values('avg_rating', ascending=False)

# Get the top decade
top_decade = decade_stats_sorted.iloc[0]

result = {
    'top_decade': top_decade['decade_label'],
    'avg_rating': round(float(top_decade['avg_rating']), 2),
    'num_books': int(top_decade['num_books']),
    'all_decades': decade_stats_sorted.to_dict('records')
}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result, indent=2))

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:6.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).
